content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 8, 1977

Mr. David N. M. Berk
Attorney at Law
874 Beaumont Avenue
Beaumont, California 92223

Dear Mr. Berk:

Thank you for your letter of May 26, 1977. You inquire
whether a conflict of interest arises where a member of a
city planning commission announces his intention to run for
the city council of the city, of which he is a member of that
planning commission.

Based on the facts above, there is no conflict of interest
under the Political Reform Act. Government Code §87100 provides:

No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, partici-
pate in making or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial
interest. [Emphasis added.]

No financial interest arises because of one's quest for
election or appointment to public office, and it is only finan-
cial interests which are made the subject of disqualification
pursuant to Government Code §87100 (see Government Code §87103).
Likewise, the mere holding of multiple public offices does not
raise the spectre of conflict of interest under the Act.

If there are other facts which lead you to believe that a
potential conflict exists under the Political Reform Act, please
detail the nature of those facts and identify the official
whose duties are in question.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION: (916) 445-4848 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL DIVISION: (916) 445-1288 · ENFORCEMENT COMPLAINT DISTRICT 916 2444

David N. M. Berk
June 8, 1977
Page Two


     I trust that these comments are responsive to your
question. If you have further questions, please feel
free to call on me at 916/322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:glb

LAW OFFICES

DAVID N. M. BERK

DAVID N. M. BERK
JOSEPH C. LUNSFORD, JR.

824 BEAUMONT AVENUE
POST OFFICE BOX 427
BEAUMONT, CALIFORNIA 92223
TELEPHONE (714) 845-2161

May 26, 1977

Mr. Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.,
Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
1100 K Street Building,
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

Thank you for sending me your memo on Recent Developments in the
Conflict of Interest Codes Under the Political Reform Act of
California.

I was particularly interested in the letter you wrote Walter
Colby, County Counsel of the County of Yuba, regarding responsibilities of members of the planning commission.

I would greatly appreciate your opinion on whether or not a
conflict of interest arises where a member of the city planning
commission announces his intention to run for the city council
of the city, of which he is a member of that planning commission,
or whether such announced intention would be an inappropriate
action for a member of the planning commission.

Your early reply will be appreciated.

Sincerely,

DAVID N. M. BERK

DNMB/sab

sections.question_synthetic: Does a conflict of interest arise under the Political Reform Act when a city planning commission member announces his candidacy for city council in the same city?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest arises under the Political Reform Act because seeking election to another office does not create a financial interest subject to disqualification, and holding multiple public offices does not inherently constitute a conflict.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality is poor with numerous character recognition errors (e.g., 'intezes' for 'interest', 'tinah~ cial' for 'financial'). Question section reconstructed from incoming request at end of document. Both the incoming request (May 26, 1977) and FPPC response (June 8, 1977) are included in this document.

embedding.summary: The FPPC Chief of the Conflicts of Interest Division advises that a planning commission member's announcement of candidacy for city council does not create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, as financial interests—not political ambitions—trigger disqualification requirements.
